critic_findings.message: set -uo pipefail is missing -e flag; all bash scripts in this repo use set -euo pipefail per convention
set -uo pipefail is missing -e flag; all bash scripts in this repo use set -euo pipefail per convention

findings_details.title: Rule 1 bypass: |& (stderr+stdout pipe) not detected
findings_details.body: The regex `\|[[:space:]]*(head|tail|grep)` matches literal `|` but not bash's `|&` (which redirects both stdout and stderr). `bin/test |& tail -5` exits 0 (allowed). A subagent can use |& to truncate gate output without triggering the block.

findings_details.title: Rule 2 bypass: git -C <dir> rebase -i not detected
findings_details.body: The Rule 2 regex `git[[:space:]]+rebase` requires `git` followed immediately by spaces then `rebase`. `git -C /tmp rebase -i HEAD~3` inserts `-C /tmp` between `git` and `rebase`, so the regex does not match and the command is allowed. A subagent can run interactive rebase in any working directory using -C.

findings_details.title: Rule 3 bypass: git -c <key>=<val> commit --amend not detected
findings_details.body: The Rule 3 regex `git[[:space:]]+commit` requires `git` followed directly by spaces then `commit`. `git -c user.name=x commit --amend` inserts a `-c` config override between `git` and `commit`, bypassing the regex. Confirmed: exits 0.

findings_details.title: Rule 4 bypass: git -C <dir> push --force not detected
findings_details.body: Same class of bypass as rules 2 and 3. The Rule 4 regex `git[[:space:]]+push` does not account for global git options like `-C`, `-c`, or `--git-dir`. `git -C /tmp push --force origin main` exits 0. A subagent can force-push to any remote from any working dir using -C.

findings_details.title: Rules 2, 3, 4 regex matches banned patterns inside string arguments — false positives block legitimate commits
findings_details.body: The grep-based regex for rules 2 (rebase -i), 3 (--amend), and 4 (--force) matches the banned pattern anywhere in the command string, including inside quoted arguments. For example, `git commit -m "fix: remove git push --force from script"` is blocked by rule 4 because the regex finds `git push --force` inside the commit message string. Similarly, `git commit -m "disabling git rebase -i"` triggers rule 2. This causes false-positive blocks when agents write commit messages that describe what they're fixing. Fix: anchor rule 4's regex with a leading word/token boundary so it only fires when `git push --force` appears as a shell token, not inside a string. One approach: strip shell-quoted strings before running the regex check, or require the git command to appear at the start of a statement (after `^`, `;`, `&&`, `||`, or `|`). Same fix needed for rules 2 and 3.

findings_details.title: SubagentStart dispatcher bypasses hook-lib.sh — violates the dispatcher architecture invariant
findings_details.body: All other dispatchers (rq-session-dispatcher.sh, rq-prompt-submit-dispatcher.sh, rq-bash-pre-dispatcher.sh, rq-we-pre-dispatcher.sh) source hook-lib.sh and use hook_run_guard to invoke sub-guards. The new rq-subagent-start-dispatcher.sh shells out directly to the sub-guard (`bash "${SCRIPT_DIR}/cardinal-rules-inject.sh" </dev/null`) without sourcing hook-lib.sh or calling hook_run_guard. This violates .review/correctness/hook-dispatcher-architecture.md rule 3: "Dispatchers source hook-lib.sh and use its helpers. New dispatchers that bypass these helpers are a fail." It also means the SubagentStart dispatcher produces no perf log rows, so the hook-trace skill and performance monitoring see a blind spot. Fix: source hook-lib.sh, then use `hook_run_guard` to call cardinal-rules-inject.sh and aggregate the additionalContext from its output into a top-level JSON object.

findings_details.title: Tests do not cover the false-positive scenario — no test for banned pattern inside a quoted commit message
findings_details.body: The test suite covers happy paths (blocks the banned command, allows the allowed command) but has no test for the false-positive scenario where a banned pattern like `--force` or `--amend` appears inside a quoted commit message or string argument. Adding `expect_allow 'git commit -m "fix: remove git push --force"' "force in commit message"` would catch regression to the current buggy behavior. Without this test, fixing the regex has no regression guard.
